title: Actor Model & Processes
question: What is the actor model and how do Erlang's lightweight processes implement it?
answer: The actor model treats concurrent computation as isolated actors that hold private state and communicate only by asynchronous messages. In Erlang each actor is a lightweight BEAM process with its own heap and stack, sharing nothing with other processes and interacting only through message passing.
explanation: Because processes share no memory, there are no locks or data races; state changes only via received messages. BEAM processes are extremely cheap (a few hundred bytes) and scheduled by the VM, so a single node can run millions of them, making massive concurrency practical.
codeExample:
% Each process is isolated with its own memory
Pid = spawn(fun() ->
    receive
        {greet, Name} -> io:format("Hello ~s~n", [Name])
    end
end),
Pid ! {greet, "world"}.  % send a message to the actor
bestPractices: Keep each process responsible for a single piece of state.
Communicate only through messages, never shared memory.
commonMistakes: Treating Erlang processes like OS threads that share memory.
Building a monolithic process instead of many small actors.
interviewTips: Stress share-nothing isolation as the source of concurrency safety.
Note processes are VM-scheduled, not OS threads.

title: spawn, self & register
question: How do spawn, self, and register work when creating and naming processes?
answer: spawn/1,3 starts a new process and returns its Pid, self/0 returns the calling process's own Pid, and register/2 associates an atom name with a Pid so other processes can reach it without knowing the raw Pid. Sends can then target the registered name.
explanation: Pids are the address used for message passing, and self() is how a process tells another where to reply. register gives long-lived service processes a stable, global-per-node name so callers do not have to thread the Pid around; whereis/1 looks the name back up.
codeExample:
loop() ->
    receive
        {From, inc} -> From ! {ok, 1}, loop()
    end.

start() ->
    Pid = spawn(fun loop/0),
    register(counter, Pid),   % give it a stable name
    counter ! {self(), inc},  % send via registered name
    receive {ok, N} -> N end.
bestPractices: Register only long-lived, singleton service processes.
Pass self() in a message so the receiver knows where to reply.
commonMistakes: Registering short-lived processes and leaking stale names.
Assuming a registered name survives the process crashing.
interviewTips: Explain that register gives a node-local atom alias for a Pid.
Mention whereis/1 to resolve a name back to a Pid.

title: Message Passing & Mailboxes
question: How does asynchronous message passing and the process mailbox work?
answer: Sending with Pid ! Msg is asynchronous and never blocks; the message is copied into the receiver's mailbox, an ordered queue. The receiver uses a receive block to pull messages out, blocking until a matching one arrives or an after timeout fires.
explanation: Messages are copied (not shared) to preserve isolation, and delivery order between two given processes is preserved. The mailbox decouples sender and receiver in time, which is the basis of Erlang's resilient asynchronous design.
codeExample:
Pid ! hello,          % async send, returns immediately
receive
    hello -> ok;
    {data, X} -> {got, X}
after 5000 ->
    timeout            % give up after 5 seconds
end.
bestPractices: Always include an after clause when a reply may never come.
Tag messages so the receiver can pattern match them reliably.
commonMistakes: Assuming send blocks or confirms delivery.
Letting unmatched messages pile up and grow the mailbox.
interviewTips: Explain messages are copied to preserve isolation.
Note send is fire-and-forget with ordered delivery per pair.

title: Selective Receive
question: What is selective receive and what are its performance implications?
answer: Selective receive lets a process pull a specific message out of its mailbox by pattern, skipping non-matching ones which stay queued in order. This lets you prioritize or wait for a particular reply regardless of arrival order.
explanation: receive scans the mailbox from the front, testing each message against the patterns; unmatched messages are left in place. If the mailbox is large and the awaited message is rare, this scan can become O(n) per receive, so references or the after 0 pattern are used to bound the cost.
codeExample:
%% Wait specifically for our tagged reply, ignore others for now
Ref = make_ref(),
Server ! {request, self(), Ref},
receive
    {reply, Ref, Result} -> Result   % matched even if queued later
after 5000 ->
    timeout
end.
bestPractices: Use a unique make_ref() tag to match exactly the reply you want.
Keep mailboxes small to avoid expensive scans.
commonMistakes: Leaving unmatched messages that force repeated full scans.
Ignoring the O(n) cost of selective receive on large mailboxes.
interviewTips: Explain that non-matching messages remain queued in order.
Mention the reference-tag trick to bound the scan.

title: Pattern Matching
question: How does pattern matching drive control flow and data destructuring in Erlang?
answer: Pattern matching binds variables by matching a value against a structural pattern; a failed match raises an error. It powers assignment with =, function head selection, case expressions, and receive clauses, destructuring tuples and lists in one step.
explanation: The = operator is a match, not assignment: it succeeds only if the shapes align, binding unbound variables. Function clauses and case branches are chosen by the first matching pattern, replacing much conditional logic with declarative structure matching.
codeExample:
{ok, Value} = {ok, 42},         % binds Value = 42
[Head | Tail] = [1, 2, 3],       % Head = 1, Tail = [2,3]
area(Shape) ->
    case Shape of
        {circle, R}  -> 3.14159 * R * R;
        {rect, W, H} -> W * H
    end.
bestPractices: Destructure data in the function head instead of nested case.
Match on tagged tuples like {ok, X} and {error, Reason}.
commonMistakes: Expecting = to reassign an already-bound variable.
Forgetting a clause and hitting a function_clause crash.
interviewTips: Describe = as a match operation, not assignment.
Show matching selecting function clauses.

title: Immutability & Single Assignment
question: What do immutability and single assignment mean in Erlang?
answer: Every variable can be bound exactly once within its scope (single assignment), and all data is immutable, so values never change after creation. Operations that seem to modify data actually return new values.
explanation: Immutability removes shared-state hazards, which is essential for safe concurrency since processes copy data rather than mutate it. Single assignment makes code easier to reason about and enables cheap structural sharing of unchanged parts of terms.
codeExample:
X = 10,
%% X = 20,  %% error: no match of right hand side value 20
Y = X + 5,          % Y = 15, X still 10
List  = [1, 2, 3],
List2 = [0 | List], % new list; List is unchanged
{X, Y, List2}.
bestPractices: Use new variable names (X1, X2) or pipelines instead of reassigning.
Rely on immutability to reason about concurrent code safely.
commonMistakes: Trying to rebind a variable and hitting a match error.
Thinking list or tuple operations mutate the original.
interviewTips: Link immutability to safe message copying between processes.
Explain single assignment enables structural sharing.

title: Atoms, Tuples & Lists
question: What are atoms, tuples, and lists and when do you use each?
answer: Atoms are named constants (like ok or error) used as tags and identifiers. Tuples are fixed-size ordered collections written {a, b, c} for grouping a known number of values. Lists are variable-length sequences written [1, 2, 3] built from cons cells.
explanation: Atoms are interned and compared in constant time, ideal as status tags in tagged tuples such as {ok, Value}. Tuples give O(1) access by position for fixed records of data, while lists are optimized for head prepend and sequential traversal. Choosing the right one shapes performance and readability.
codeExample:
Status = ok,                       % atom
Person = {person, "Alice", 30},    % tagged tuple
Nums   = [1, 2, 3 | [4, 5]],       % list, cons syntax
{person, Name, Age} = Person,      % destructure the tuple
First = hd(Nums).                  % head of the list
bestPractices: Use tagged tuples like {ok, X} to label results.
Prepend to lists (O(1)) rather than appending (O(n)).
commonMistakes: Creating unbounded dynamic atoms and exhausting the atom table.
Appending to the end of large lists in a loop.
interviewTips: Note atoms are interned and never garbage collected.
Explain lists are linked cons cells, cheap at the head.

title: Guards
question: What are guards and what expressions are allowed in them?
answer: Guards are extra boolean conditions attached to clause heads with when, refining pattern matching. A clause is chosen only if its pattern matches and its guard succeeds. Guards may use only side-effect-free built-in tests like comparisons, type checks, and arithmetic.
explanation: Guards keep clause selection pure and safe by forbidding arbitrary function calls that could have side effects or fail unexpectedly; a guard that errors simply fails silently. Commas separate ANDed conditions and semicolons separate ORed guard sequences.
bestPractices: Use type-test BIFs like is_integer/1 to narrow clauses.
Prefer guards over case-inside-a-clause for selection.
commonMistakes: Calling arbitrary user functions inside a guard.
Confusing comma (AND) with semicolon (OR) in guard sequences.
interviewTips: List allowed guard BIFs and comparison operators.
Explain a failing guard just skips the clause.

title: Records
question: What are records in Erlang and how are they implemented?
answer: Records give named fields to tuples using the -record directive, so you can access data by field name instead of position. At compile time the compiler rewrites record syntax into plain tuple operations, adding no runtime overhead.
explanation: A record #user{...} is really a tuple whose first element is the record tag atom. The compiler expands field access and updates into tuple element/setelement calls, so records are ergonomic sugar over tuples. Because the definition is compile-time, records are shared via header (.hrl) files, and maps are often preferred for dynamic data.
codeExample:
-record(user, {name, age = 0, email}).

U  = #user{name = "Bob", age = 25},
Name = U#user.name,             % field access
U2  = U#user{age = 26},         % returns a new record
#user{email = E} = U2.          % pattern match a field
bestPractices: Give record fields sensible defaults in the definition.
Share record definitions through .hrl header files.
commonMistakes: Assuming records are a runtime type rather than tuple sugar.
Using records where maps would handle dynamic keys better.
interviewTips: Explain records compile down to tagged tuples.
Contrast records (compile-time) with maps (runtime).

title: List Comprehensions
question: How do list comprehensions with generators and filters work?
answer: A list comprehension builds a new list from an expression evaluated over one or more generators (Pattern <- List), optionally constrained by filter conditions. Multiple generators produce a cartesian product of combinations.
explanation: Generators bind each element in turn, filters (boolean expressions) drop elements that fail, and the head expression transforms survivors into the result. They express map and filter declaratively in a single concise construct and can nest several generators for combinatorial results.
bestPractices: Combine map and filter into one comprehension for clarity.
Order filters before expensive expressions to short-circuit work.
commonMistakes: Expecting a generator variable to leak outside the comprehension.
Building huge intermediate lists instead of streaming.
interviewTips: Explain generators, filters, and the head expression.
Show multiple generators forming a cartesian product.

title: Funs (Anonymous Functions)
question: What are funs and how do closures capture their environment?
answer: A fun is an anonymous function value created with fun ... end that can be passed around, stored, and called later. Funs are closures: they capture the values of variables in scope at creation time, which stay immutable inside the fun.
explanation: Funs enable higher-order programming with lists:map, lists:foldl, and callbacks. Because captured variables are immutable, the closed-over environment is stable and safe to send to other processes. Funs can also reference named functions via fun Module:Name/Arity.
codeExample:
Double = fun(X) -> X * 2 end,
lists:map(Double, [1, 2, 3]),      % [2, 4, 6]

Adder = fun(N) -> fun(X) -> X + N end end,
Add5  = Adder(5),                  % closure captures N = 5
Add5(10).                          % 15
bestPractices: Pass funs to lists:map/foldl for concise transformations.
Use fun Mod:Fun/Arity references for hot-code-upgradable callbacks.
commonMistakes: Expecting captured variables to change after the fun is created.
Capturing large terms in a fun sent across processes.
interviewTips: Explain closures capture immutable values at definition time.
Mention named-fun references survive code upgrades better.

title: Recursion & Tail Recursion
question: Why is tail recursion important in Erlang and how does it work?
answer: Erlang has no loops, so iteration is expressed with recursion. A tail-recursive function makes its recursive call as the very last action, letting the VM reuse the current stack frame (tail-call optimization) so it runs in constant stack space.
explanation: Non-tail recursion grows the stack per call and can exhaust memory on long inputs. By threading an accumulator and calling itself last, a function loops indefinitely in constant space, which is essential for long-running server loops that recurse forever to keep state.
codeExample:
%% Tail-recursive sum using an accumulator
sum(List) -> sum(List, 0).

sum([], Acc) -> Acc;
sum([H | T], Acc) -> sum(T, Acc + H).  % last call = tail call

%% sum([1,2,3,4]) => 10, constant stack
bestPractices: Use an accumulator to make recursion tail-recursive.
Write server loops as infinite tail-recursive functions.
commonMistakes: Doing work after the recursive call, breaking tail-call optimization.
Reversing accumulators too often instead of once at the end.
interviewTips: Explain last-call optimization reusing the stack frame.
Contrast body recursion vs tail recursion in stack use.

title: Links & Monitors
question: How do links and monitors differ for observing process lifecycles?
answer: A link is a bidirectional connection: if one linked process dies abnormally, an exit signal propagates to the other, killing it too unless it traps exits. A monitor is unidirectional and non-invasive: the monitoring process just receives a 'DOWN' message when the monitored one exits.
explanation: Links are the foundation of supervision because a crash naturally propagates so failures are noticed and handled together. Monitors are ideal when you only want to observe another process without your own fate being tied to it, since they never kill the observer and carry a unique reference.
codeExample:
%% Link: crash propagates (unless trapping exits)
spawn_link(fun() -> exit(boom) end),

%% Monitor: just get notified, no propagation
Ref = monitor(process, Pid),
receive
    {'DOWN', Ref, process, Pid, Reason} -> {died, Reason}
end.
bestPractices: Use monitors when you only need to observe, not be affected.
Set process_flag(trap_exit, true) in processes that manage links.
commonMistakes: Using a link where a monitor's one-way semantics is safer.
Forgetting to demonitor and leaking stale monitors.
interviewTips: Contrast bidirectional links with unidirectional monitors.
Explain trap_exit converting exit signals into messages.

title: Error Handling & Exit Signals
question: How do try/catch and exit signals handle errors in Erlang?
answer: try/catch handles the three exception classes: throw (user-raised via throw/1), error (runtime failures like badarg), and exit (process termination via exit/1). Exit signals additionally propagate between linked processes, and trapping exits turns them into ordinary {'EXIT', ...} messages.
explanation: You match on Class:Reason in the catch section to react to specific failures, but idiomatic Erlang catches sparingly and lets unexpected errors crash. Exit signals are a separate inter-process mechanism carried over links; a process with trap_exit set receives them as messages instead of dying.
bestPractices: Catch only exceptions you can meaningfully handle.
Use trap_exit in supervisory processes to convert signals to messages.
commonMistakes: Wrapping everything in try/catch instead of letting it crash.
Confusing throw (control flow) with error and exit classes.
interviewTips: Name the three exception classes: throw, error, exit.
Explain trap_exit turning exit signals into messages.

title: gen_server Behaviour
question: What is the gen_server behaviour and which callbacks does it require?
answer: gen_server is an OTP behaviour that abstracts the generic server loop, receive handling, and state management, letting you implement only callbacks. You provide init/1, handle_call/3 for synchronous requests, handle_cast/2 for asynchronous ones, and optionally handle_info/2, terminate/2, and code_change/3.
explanation: The behaviour module handles the message loop, timeouts, and system messages, while your callbacks return tuples like {reply, R, State} or {noreply, State} to drive it. This separation gives you a battle-tested, uniform server with built-in support for supervision, tracing, and hot upgrades.
codeExample:
-module(counter).
-behaviour(gen_server).
-export([start_link/0, init/1, handle_call/3, handle_cast/2]).

start_link() -> gen_server:start_link({local, ?MODULE}, ?MODULE, 0, []).
init(N) -> {ok, N}.
handle_call(get, _From, N) -> {reply, N, N};
handle_call(_, _, N) -> {reply, ok, N}.
handle_cast(inc, N) -> {noreply, N + 1}.
bestPractices: Use handle_call for synchronous replies and handle_cast for fire-and-forget.
Keep callback state small and let the behaviour run the loop.
commonMistakes: Doing long blocking work inside handle_call and stalling the server.
Returning a malformed tuple that crashes the gen_server.
interviewTips: List the core callbacks and their return tuples.
Explain call (sync) vs cast (async) semantics.

title: Hot Code Swapping
question: How does hot code swapping let you upgrade a running system?
answer: The BEAM keeps two versions of a module (current and old) loaded at once. A fully-qualified call like Module:function(...) always dispatches to the newest loaded version, so a running process picks up new code the next time it makes such a call, without stopping the system.
explanation: Local (unqualified) calls stay in the same module version, while external calls jump to the latest. Reloading a module purges the oldest version and kills any process still lingering in it. OTP's release upgrades and gen_server's code_change/3 build on this to migrate state safely during upgrades.
codeExample:
loop(State) ->
    receive
        upgrade ->
            ?MODULE:loop(State);   % qualified call jumps to new code
        Msg ->
            loop(handle(Msg, State))
    end.
%% shell: c(mymod).  then send 'upgrade' to adopt the new version
bestPractices: Trigger upgrades via fully-qualified ?MODULE: calls in loops.
Use gen_server code_change/3 to transform state on upgrade.
commonMistakes: Assuming a running loop adopts new code without an external call.
Loading a module twice and purging processes still in old code.
interviewTips: Explain the two-version model and qualified vs local calls.
Mention code_change/3 for state migration.

title: Let It Crash & Supervision
question: What is the 'let it crash' philosophy and how do supervision trees implement it?
answer: 'Let it crash' means workers should not defensively handle every error but fail fast and cleanly on unexpected conditions, leaving recovery to dedicated supervisors. A supervisor is an OTP process that starts, monitors, and restarts child processes according to a restart strategy, and supervisors nest into a supervision tree.
explanation: Crashing frees a process from a corrupt state and lets a supervisor restart it in a known-good initial state, so the system self-heals. Strategies like one_for_one, one_for_all, and rest_for_one decide which siblings restart, and restart intensity limits prevent endless crash loops by escalating failures up the tree.
codeExample:
-module(my_sup).
-behaviour(supervisor).
-export([init/1]).

init([]) ->
    Child = #{id => worker,
              start => {worker, start_link, []},
              restart => permanent},
    Flags = #{strategy => one_for_one, intensity => 5, period => 10},
    {ok, {Flags, [Child]}}.
bestPractices: Keep workers simple and let supervisors own recovery.
Tune restart intensity/period to catch crash loops early.
commonMistakes: Adding defensive try/catch that hides real failures.
Putting business logic in supervisors instead of workers.
interviewTips: Explain restarting into a clean known state as self-healing.
Name the restart strategies and intensity limits.

title: ETS Tables
question: What are ETS tables and when should you use them?
answer: ETS (Erlang Term Storage) provides fast, in-memory key-value tables that live outside process heaps and can hold large amounts of data with constant-time access. They come in types like set, ordered_set, bag, and duplicate_bag, with configurable access (private, protected, public).
explanation: Because processes share nothing, ETS is the idiomatic way to share large or frequently read state across processes without copying it in messages. Tables are owned by the process that creates them and vanish when that owner dies (unless given away), and concurrent reads/writes are handled by the VM without user locks.
codeExample:
Tab = ets:new(users, [set, public, named_table]),
ets:insert(users, {1, "Alice"}),
ets:insert(users, {2, "Bob"}),
[{1, Name}] = ets:lookup(users, 1),   % O(1) lookup
ets:delete(users, 1).
bestPractices: Use ETS for large shared read-mostly state instead of message copies.
Pick the table type (set/ordered_set/bag) to match access needs.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the table dies with its owning process.
Assuming ETS data is immutable like normal Erlang terms.
interviewTips: Explain ETS lives outside process heaps to avoid copying.
Contrast the set, ordered_set, and bag table types.

title: Distribution & Nodes
question: How does Erlang distribution let processes communicate across nodes?
answer: An Erlang node is a running BEAM instance with a name; nodes connect into a mesh and exchange messages transparently. Sending to a remote Pid or {Name, Node} works exactly like local send, giving location transparency across a cluster.
explanation: Nodes authenticate using a shared magic cookie and, once connected, form a fully connected mesh where Pids carry their node of origin. spawn/4 can start processes on remote nodes, and the same message-passing semantics apply, so distributed systems are built with the same primitives as local concurrency, though network partitions and latency must be considered.
codeExample:
%% start: erl -name a@host -setcookie secret
net_kernel:connect_node('b@host'),
nodes(),                              % list connected nodes
Pid = spawn('b@host', fun() -> work() end),  % remote spawn
{logger, 'b@host'} ! {log, "hello"}.  % send to remote registered name
bestPractices: Secure clusters with a strong shared cookie and network controls.
Design for partitions and latency, not just local semantics.
commonMistakes: Assuming remote sends are as reliable and fast as local ones.
Leaving the default cookie, exposing the cluster.
interviewTips: Explain location transparency and the magic cookie.
Note the fully connected mesh and its scaling limits.

title: The BEAM VM
question: What is the BEAM VM and how does its scheduler support massive concurrency?
answer: BEAM is the Erlang virtual machine that executes compiled bytecode and manages processes, memory, and scheduling. It runs one scheduler thread per CPU core, each preemptively multiplexing many lightweight processes, so millions of processes share a handful of OS threads efficiently.
explanation: BEAM uses reduction counting: each process runs until it consumes a budget of reductions (roughly function calls) and is then preempted, guaranteeing fairness and soft real-time responsiveness even under load. Per-process heaps enable independent, low-latency garbage collection, and schedulers balance work by migrating processes across cores.
codeExample:
erlang:system_info(schedulers),          % scheduler threads (per core)
erlang:system_info(process_count),        % live processes
Pid = spawn(fun() -> ok end),
erlang:process_info(self(), reductions).  % this process's reductions
bestPractices: Trust preemptive scheduling for fairness under heavy load.
Keep processes small so per-process GC stays fast.
commonMistakes: Assuming a busy process can starve others (reductions prevent it).
Confusing BEAM processes with OS threads.
interviewTips: Explain reduction-based preemptive scheduling.
Mention per-process heaps enabling isolated, low-latency GC.
